title: Violations of the Leggett-Garg inequality in Hybrid Liouvillian Dynamics: The Nonlinear Role of Detector Efficiency
authors: Sourav Paul, Parveen Kumar, Sourin Das
abstract: Violations of the Leggett-Garg inequality (LGI) up to its algebraic bound under non-Hermitian dynamics are well established theoretically. Here, we demonstrate that such extreme violations are intrinsically fragile when realistic measurement processes are taken into account. We consider an open two-level system described by a time-local hybrid Liouvillian, with a continuous parameter $q \in [0,1]$, representing detector efficiency, i.e., the fraction of quantum jump trajectories that are retained in the ensemble. This parameter interpolates between trace-preserving Lindblad dynamics ($q=1$) and non-Hermitian ``no-jump" evolution ($q=0$). While $K_3$ approaches its algebraic maximum of 3 in the null-efficiency limit, even an infinitesimal increase in detector efficiency induces a rapid, highly nonlinear suppression toward the classical bound. This logarithmic sensitivity reveals that maximal LGI violations are not robust physical features but rather singular limits of idealized measurement conditions. Our results have direct experimental implications: achieving algebraic LGI violations in systems undergoing continuous time evolution requires near-perfect suppression of detected quantum jumps (i.e., effective post-selection), placing stringent constraints on detector performance. In contrast to discrete protocols based on time-non-divisible dynamics, our framework shows that extreme violations arising within continuous, divisible quantum trajectory evolution constitute a fundamentally fragile regime.
summary: The paper shows that the extreme violations of the Leggett-Garg inequality predicted in non-Hermitian dynamics are not robust but are highly sensitive to detector efficiency. It reveals a nonlinear, logarithmic collapse of these violations as soon as any quantum jumps are detected, placing strict constraints on experimental implementations.
main_problem: Investigating the robustness of extreme violations of the Leggett-Garg inequality (LGI) under realistic measurement conditions and determining if super-quantum violations are physically achievable or merely singular limits.
main_result: Extreme LGI violations (approaching the algebraic bound of 3) are found to be intrinsically fragile, as even an infinitesimal increase in detector efficiency causes a rapid, nonlinear suppression of the violation toward the classical bound.
method: The authors use a quantum trajectory framework with a non-trace-preserving, time-local hybrid Liouvillian master equation and analyze sequential measurement protocols and joint probabilities.
model_or_system: An open two-level system (qubit) undergoing hybrid Liouvanian dynamics, characterized by a Hamiltonian with coherent tunneling and a dissipation term that includes a detector efficiency parameter q.
key_observables: Leggett-Garg parameter (K3), No-Signaling in Time (NSIT) conditions, Arrow of Time (AoT) conditions, and Bloch vector components.
important_parameters: Detector efficiency (q), dissipation rate (gamma), coupling strength (J), and the dimensionless decay rate (gamma/J).
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be time-local and Markovian within the hybrid Liouvillian framework.
figures_summary: The figures include a heatmap of the maximal K3 parameter and a universal scaling profile showing the hyperbolic-tangent dependence of the violation on the logarithm of detector efficiency.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of LGI violations, derives the hybrid Liouvillian master equation from a microscopic model, analyzes the spectral structure and exceptional points, quantifies the violation of LGI and NSIT conditions, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics because it demonstrates how continuous monitoring and imperfect post-selection can fundamentally alter temporal correlations and suppress non-classicality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Maximal violation of the LG parameter. (a) The maximal value Kmax 3 is shown as a function of the detector efficiency parameter q (displayed on a logarithmic scale) and the dissipation ratio γ/J. The colormap encodes the magnitude of Kmax 3 , highlighting the parameter regions where the violation exceeds the L¨uders bound. The green curve marks the boundary at K3 = 1.5: the region below this line corresponds to super-quantum violation, while the region above corresponds to the quantum-allowed domain. The white dashed curve denotes the exceptional line of the hybrid Liouvillian [Eq. (1)], with further discussion provided in Appendix C. (b) The corresponding gradient of Kmax 3 over...

FIG. 2. Bloch vector trajectories under hybrid Liouvillian dynamics. Evolution of the quantum state projected onto the ˆy-ˆz plane, initialized in the |+⟩y state. System parameters are fixed at J = 1, γ = 0.9905. Black and red markers indicate the initial (t = 0) and asymptotic (t →∞) states, respectively. Blue markers trace the intermediate temporal evolution, while green arrows depict the instantaneous flow direction of the trajectory. Plots correspond to varying parameter values arranged from left to right: q ∈{0, 10−5, 10−3, 10−2, 10−1, 1}. A distinct qualitative transition in the dynamical topology is observable as q increases.

FIG. 3. Fitted curve for Kmax 3 . Plot.(a) displays Kmax 3 as a function of the parameters γ and q. The interval 1 ≤γ ≤2 is not included, as this range exhibits no notable structure relevant for the fitting. Plot.(b) illustrates the resulting fit, where Kmax 3 (q, γ) is modeled as a polynomial function of γ (up to twentieth order) combined with a hyperbolic-tangent dependence on log q . This composite form captures the rapid variation in log q and the nonlinear dependence on γ. Plot.(c) shows the accuracy of the fit — the absolute value difference of plot.(a) and plot.(b) is captured here. The black bold dotted line divides the plot into two regions — on an overall scenario, Region 1 shows...

FIG. 4. Violation of two and three-time NSIT conditions: (a) Plot of a specific component of ∆0(1)2 as a function of the parameters γ and q, corresponding to the dichotomic measurements q0 = +1 at time t0 = 0 and q2 = +1 at time t2 = 2t. (b) Plot illustrates a specific component of ∆(i)j for i = 1 and j = 2, given the dichotomic measurement q2 = +1 at time t2 = 2t. Further details are provided in Appendix E.
